---
title: "Compose distributions into event trees"
description: "Wire delays into a tree structure with Sequential, Parallel, Resolve, and Compete — one object that simulates and scores a record."
toc: true
toc-location: left
---

:::::: {.side-space}

## The composed way of thinking

A case's progression through detection and care is a tree of delays. A single
composed distribution describes the whole record — the same object that
simulates a case's event path with `rand` also scores an observed record with
`logpdf`. The object is dual-purpose: it is the model in both directions.

The tree below builds two parallel pathways from onset — a clinical chain
(admission then resolution via death or discharge) and a surveillance chain
(notification then a competing-hazard outcome). This mirrors the structure of
a real Ebola line-list analysis, where four delays describe a case's
progression through detection and care, with censoring, case-fatality
covariates, and posterior recovery all handled through the one composed object.

:::

::: {.code-example-column .section-start-space .section-end-space}
::: {.code-window .box}
::: {.code-window-header}
::: {.code-window-dots}

```{=html}
<span class="dot red"></span><span class="dot yellow"></span><span class="dot green"></span>
```

:::

::: {.code-window-title}

```{=html}
<span>composed_delays.jl</span>
```

:::

:::

```{.julia .code-overflow-scroll}
using ComposedDistributions, Distributions

# BDBV-inspired delay tree: parallel pathways
# with Sequential, Resolve, and Compete nested together
tree = compose((
    clinical = sequential(
        :onset_admit => Gamma(1.2, 3.0),
        :admit_resolve => resolve(
            :death => (Gamma(2.0, 3.5), 0.3),
            :discharge => (Gamma(1.0, 8.0), 0.7))),
    surveillance = sequential(
        :onset_notif => Gamma(0.7, 20.0),
        :notif_compete => compete(
            :confirmed => Gamma(2.0, 1.0),
            :lost => Gamma(5.0, 0.5))),
))

# Inspect tree, params, and default priors
event_tree(tree)
params_table(tree)
priors = build_priors(params_table(tree))

# Simulate a record and score it
rand(tree)
logpdf(tree, ans)
```

:::
:::

## Why compose distributions?

**One object, two directions.** The same tree is generative and analytic.
`rand(tree)` draws a named event record; `logpdf(tree, record)` scores it.
The model is built once and used in both directions — no separate simulator.

**Named events.** Every delay in the tree has a label — `onset_admit`, `death`,
`discharge`, `notif_compete` — so the record structure is self-documenting.
The flat event names are the columns a data row supplies, and a missing column
drives whether that delay is conditioned on or marginalised.

**Flat parameters.** `params_table` inventories every free parameter in one
table keyed by edge path, with the support a prior must respect.
`build_priors` derives a prior per parameter from that support (positive shape
→ positive-truncated Normal), then partial overrides are done by path — name
only the leaves you care about.

**Composers cover every event relationship.** Five composers wire delays
together:
- `Sequential` — conjunctive chain (steps add up)
- `Parallel` — independent branches off a shared origin
- `Resolve` — fixed-probability disjunction (one outcome, known split)
- `Compete` — racing hazards (which outcome occurs is coupled to when)
- `Choose` — data-selected disjunction (a field picks the sub-model)

Trees nest freely: a `Resolve` node is a valid step in a `Sequential` chain,
and a `compose` result drops into another `compose` as a branch.

**Censoring is part of the tree.** Each leaf can be wrapped in
`double_interval_censored` (a one-day primary and secondary window), so the
censoring is carried inside the composed object rather than handled as a
separate step. This is what makes the line-list analysis fit without per-row
likelihood wrangling.

## Fitting the line list

A full analysis builds the tree once as a template, then fits it to a set of
records through the vectorised `composed_distribution_model` interface. Each
row supplies the event columns and (for the resolve node) per-case branch
probabilities from a separate covariate model — the case-fatality ratio as a
logistic regression on health-worker status, case definition, and age. The
resolve node consumes the resulting probability through the reserved
`:branch_probs` field, and the death-versus-discharge split carries the
case-fatality information without a separate likelihood.

The posterior is read back with `update(template, chain)`, which walks the
fitted chain and rebuilds the object per draw. Each fitted delay's mean is
read off the latent fit with `mean(latent(fit))`, returning a NamedTuple
keyed by the same event names the tree was built with. The onset-to-death
delay (the convolution of two edges) comes straight from the fitted tree
without re-fitting.

## Where next

- The [ComposedDistributions.jl documentation](https://composeddistributions.epiaware.org/dev/)
  covers all five composers, structural edits (`splice`, `prune`, `tie`),
  parameter tying, and the full API.
- [CensoredDistributions.jl](https://censoreddistributions.epiaware.org/) adds
  the censoring layer that makes this approach work with real line-list data.
- Ready to fit a full model with infection dynamics? See the
  [assembled model approach](assembled-model.qmd).

::::::
